onboarding.quote: Onboarding is the only part of your product experience that a hundred percent of people are ever going to touch.
Your brand is the promise you're making and your product experience is your delivery of that promise. Those two things have to be in lockstep.
If you're not prototyping and building to see what you want to build, I think you're doing it wrong.
We put questions into the onboarding flow and it actually improved conversion. Just the act of asking questions made users feel heard.

growth.quote: Growth is the job of connecting users to the value of your product. It sometimes gets this reputation that it's just metrics hacking.
Start by assuming you no longer have product market fit, because you had product market fit in a different market.
We rarely scale a project until we know the Sean Ellis score hit a threshold that we find really compelling.
The goal of your kindle strategies, these non-scalable hacks, they only exist to unlock the things that could take you to millions of users.

retention.quote: User retention is gold for consumer subscription companies. If you don't retain your users, then a lot of the onus is on getting them to pay on day one.
In product-led sales, product acquires and activates a customer and product creates pipeline for sales.
The best way to get more people to a step is just get more people in the door in the first place. That will always hurt your conversion rate, but it may actually give you more people.

pricing.quote: The good founders need to dominate both market share and wallet share. It is not a choice.
Do not set it and forget it. Think about your pricing just like you do your roadmap. Every 6 to 12 months, there's probably something meaningful you're leaving on the table.
The most powerful unquantifiable things in the world of business are fun and delight.
What the marketplaces are selling you is taking the friction away. That's what you're paying them for.

strategy.quote: What everyone really wants is clarity. And what everyone really wants is to be able to row in the same direction really quickly.
When people say 'I want someone strategic,' what they really mean is 'I want someone who can articulate a compelling why behind the decisions.'
Way too many founders apologize for how they want to run the company. That's a good way to make everyone miserable.
The whole point about a roadmap is that it's not designed to be your plan. Think of it as a prototype for your strategy.

focus.quote: We took people away from the investment in our core product to go do those other things. The trap is that you leave yourself ripe for disruption.
It starts with adding little features here and there, making little cosmetic improvements until the next round of layoffs.
Your job is figuring out what the algorithm should be responsible for, what people are responsible for, and the framework for making those decisions.
If you start small, it forces you to think about what is the problem that I'm going to solve.

metrics.quote: The algorithms don't understand long-term effects often, nor do they understand how people might respond to it.
There is no such thing as a long feedback loop. The way you shorten it is to say, what are the things correlated with the outcome that I eventually desire?
I have an allergic reaction to vanity metrics. The transition from operator to investor made that very clear.
Most people tend to ignore the non-determinism. You don't know how the user might behave with your product.

user_research.quote: Pessimism sounds extremely sophisticated. Optimism always sounds dumb or at least naive.
I want someone that's going to champion and be a change agent to do things that may be hard but actually best for the long-term interest of the product.
If I drill down what makes companies fail, it's quite simple. They don't talk to users, which means they don't find product market fit.
It's not about being the first company to have an agent. It's about whether you've built the right flywheels to improve over time.

activation.quote: An activation rate in a lower percentage range, maybe 5 to 15%, is better than a higher one because it means much higher correlation with long-term retention.
Facebook knew it needed to get you to 10 friends in 14 days. If you got your 10 friends in 14 days, you were probably going to use Facebook for the rest of your life.
When you give away your free product, what you want in exchange is a broader reach and understanding of usage behavior.

churn.quote: Your entire customer base changed, not just the next 10% of customers that you're looking for.
If you don't have a foundation of data and understanding of how to analyze it, you are giving away a free product for nothing.
The simple act of getting together and saying 'what was the most awesome thing that happened this week' makes people feel like they're part of something special.

decision_making.quote: 40 to 60% of B2B purchase processes end in no decision. The majority couldn't figure out how to make a choice confidently.
The brain is like a college campus with different departments. Most people rely on their history department way too much.
There's one failure mode where they don't know what the basics are. Then there's another where they never test it.

positioning.quote: When you're building a category, you need to make sure there is a category validated by analysts and directory sites.
A bad name with a really great company with great strategy and marketing is going to be great over time.
What's the thing your product does? Start there. That question alone will transform how you think about AI in your product.

experimentation.quote: A prototype is essentially a way of checking your assumptions. Think about it at the strategy level.
One easy, slippery slope is to keep thinking about complexities of the solution and forget the problem that you're trying to solve.
We approached the team and said 'can we take this?' They said sure. And we immediately blew it up.

product_market_fit.quote: Start by assuming you no longer have product market fit, because you had product market fit in a different market. It's a different market now.
We would ask founders 'who exactly is your target customer?' and three co-founders have three different answers.
If I drill down what makes companies fail, it's quite simple. They don't talk to users, which means they don't find product market fit.
We called it sugar-coated broccoli. People don't want to eat the broccoli but people like sugar, so give them the sugar then hide your broccoli inside.

tech_debt.quote: The issue for us was that we took people away from the investment in our core product. The trap is you leave yourself ripe for disruption in your core.
Pain is the new moat. Going through the pain of learning, implementing, and understanding what works and what doesn't.
Every single day somebody is pushing code to production, you are making the product better or you're making the product worse.

leadership.quote: Leaders are in the details. There's this negative term called micromanagement. I think there's a difference between micromanagement and being in the details.
I do not believe in being liked. I believe in being loved. And that's a very different thing.
There is a ton of alignment built in. Something that comes from you feels a lot more familiar and easy to accept.
